doc.title: Noncounterbalancing error — expensed instead of capitalized
doc.event: At the start of year 3, Ives Co. discovers that a $50,000 machine bought two years ago (10-year life, straight-line, no salvage) was EXPENSED in full at purchase instead of capitalized. Ignore taxes. Prepare the correcting entry.

entries.caption: Correcting entry at the start of year 3

lines.label: the asset finally exists
lines.why: The machine belongs on the balance sheet at cost — putting it there is step one.
lines.trap: Noncounterbalancing means the seesaw never swings back — without correction, the books stay wrong for the asset's whole life.

lines.label: 2 years of catch-up
lines.why: Had it been capitalized, years 1–2 would each have taken 5,000 of depreciation — the accumulated account catches up to 10,000.
lines.trap: The correction rebuilds the world AS IF the error never happened — asset AND its accumulated depreciation, both.

lines.label: net prior-years effect
lines.why: Prior income was UNDER by 50,000 (the wrong expense) and OVER by 10,000 (the missing depreciation) — net understated 40,000, restored through beginning RE.
lines.trap: The RE figure is always the NET of every prior-year effect — expense error one way, missing depreciation the other. Netting wrong is where the points die.

computationPaths.narration: Reconstruct the counterfactual: what SHOULD each account show today if the purchase had been capitalized on day one?

memorize.body: Noncounterbalancing correction: rebuild every affected balance-sheet account to what it SHOULD be today; the net of all prior-year income effects plugs to Retained Earnings.
Don't forget the CURRENT year's depreciation (5,000) as a separate, ordinary entry once the asset exists — the correction fixes the past; the current year still has to happen.

questions.prompt: A 50,000 machine (10-yr SL) was expensed 2 years ago. In the start-of-year-3 correcting entry, Retained Earnings is credited for…

distractors.feedback: 50,000 restores the expense error but ignores the 10,000 of depreciation those two years SHOULD have taken. Net prior understatement: 50,000 − 10,000 = 40,000.

build.scaffold: Build the counterfactual balance sheet: asset at cost, accumulated depreciation caught up, RE takes the net. Current-year depreciation is its own ordinary entry afterward — the decoy expense accounts don't belong in the correction.
